name: Nanna Moon
description: Nanna, also known as Sin in Akkadian, was the Sumerian god of the Moon. He was the son of Enlil, the god of the air and storms, and Ninlil, the goddess of the south wind. Nanna was believed to traverse the night sky in a shining boat, illuminating the darkness. He was the father of Utu (Shamash), the sun god, and Inanna (Ishtar), the goddess of love, war, and fertility. Nanna's primary center of worship was the city of Ur, where his temple, the E-kishnugal, was located. He was associated with wisdom, knowledge, and the measurement of time, and was often depicted as an old man with a long beard. Nanna's influence extended to agriculture, as the moon's cycles were crucial for planting and harvesting. He was seen as a protector of shepherds and cattle herders, and was invoked for good fortune and prosperity. The crescent moon was his primary symbol, representing his constant waxing and waning. He was revered throughout Mesopotamia, and his worship continued for centuries.
subtitle: Sumerian God of the Moon

keyMyths.title: Nanna's Journey Across the Sky
keyMyths.description: Nanna travels across the night sky in a boat, providing light and guidance. This journey symbolizes the cyclical nature of time and the moon's phases.

keyMyths.title: The Birth of Utu and Inanna
keyMyths.description: Nanna is the father of Utu, the sun god, and Inanna, the goddess of love and war. Their birth signifies the balance of light and darkness, love and conflict.

keyMyths.title: Nanna and the City of Ur
keyMyths.description: Nanna is the patron deity of Ur, where his temple, the E-kishnugal, was located. He protected the city and its inhabitants, ensuring prosperity and well-being.

sources.author: Samuel Noah Kramer
